name: Zenith Radio Nurse
short_description: The world's first electronic baby monitor — a two-part radio system designed by Isamu Noguchi that let parents listen to their nursery from elsewhere in the home.
long_description: Introduced in March 1938, the Zenith Radio Nurse is the foundational ancestor of all domestic surveillance and home-care audio devices. Zenith president Eugene F. McDonald Jr. commissioned sculptor Isamu Noguchi to design the receiver housing after the Lindbergh kidnapping prompted concern about infant safety. The system had two components: the Guardian Ear transmitter placed near the crib and the sculptural Radio Nurse receiver placed in the parents' room, both plugging into house wiring and transmitting audio by radio over it. Noguchi's dark Bakelite receiver — a faceless, slightly sinister bust with a concealed volume wheel under its 'chin' — was included in the Whitney Museum's 1938 sculpture exhibition and now sits in the collections of MoMA, the Met, the V&A, and Cooper Hewitt. Though a commercial failure ($19.95 was prohibitive in the Depression, and signals sometimes leaked beyond the house), the Radio Nurse established the design and social logic that every subsequent baby monitor, smart speaker, and home-care camera inherits: domestic space made audible to a remote listener.
ai_note: Pure analog radio transmission — vacuum tubes, house wiring as antenna, no computation of any kind. Its intelligence is entirely in the ears of the listening parent.

images.caption: Zenith Radio Nurse (1938), Bakelite receiver designed by Isamu Noguchi, Montreal Museum of Fine Arts.
images.alt_text: A dark brown Bakelite sculpture resembling a stylized, abstract nurse's bust or head with a speaker grill on the front and no facial features, photographed against a plain background.

sources.title: Zenith Radio Nurse
sources.access_notes: Overview of product history, designer, technical operation, reception, and museum collections.

sources.title: An Industrial Design Classic: Isamu Noguchi and the Radio Nurse
sources.access_notes: Detailed technical and design history from the Henry Ford Museum collection; confirms March 1938 introduction date, house-wiring transmission method, and $19.95 retail price.

sources.title: A Modernist Mother's Helper
sources.access_notes: Cooper Hewitt essay on the Radio Nurse's design context, Noguchi's inspiration from Japanese culture, and its significance as the world's first electronic baby monitor.

sources.title: Radio Nurse | V&A Explore The Collections
sources.access_notes: V&A collection record for the Radio Nurse with dimensions and design attribution.

interactions.title: Leave the nursery and still keep an ear on the baby
interactions.description: Set the Guardian Ear transmitter by the crib, carry the Radio Nurse receiver into another room, and go about housework while the baby's sounds came through. Zenith's own pitch was 'Radio Nurse enables you to leave the nursery, yet guard your baby... Keep an "ear" on your children!'

interactions.title: Plug both halves into the wall — no installation
interactions.description: Both units simply plugged into household outlets, with the home's own electrical wiring carrying the transmitted sound between transmitter and receiver, so a parent could put the set to work without wiring or setup.

interactions.title: Tickle the chin to turn it down
interactions.description: The receiver's volume control was hidden as a wheel beneath the sculpted unit's 'chin,' so adjusting how loud the nursery came through amounted to a small tickle under its jaw.

interactions.title: Listen in on an invalid, not just an infant
interactions.description: Early Zenith advertising aimed the device at the bedside as much as the crib: an ad in the AMA's Hygeia magazine titled 'If You Have a Baby or an Invalid' pictured an elderly woman alongside a baby, framing it as a way to keep ear on a sick relative from another room.

interactions.title: Overhear the neighbors — or be overheard
interactions.description: Because the radio signal sometimes leaked beyond the house wiring, listeners could pick up strangers' voices through the receiver or have their own nursery overheard next door, an early privacy failure that discouraged adoption.

interactions.title: Crosstalk with the garage door
interactions.description: Sharing a radio frequency with other new consumer gadgets like car radios and garage-door openers meant the nursery feed could be interrupted or mingled with stray signals from around the neighborhood.

interactions.title: A second ear at the sickbed table
interactions.description: Grounded in its marketed invalid-care use, the same plug-in pair could let a caregiver doing chores downstairs hear a bedridden relative call out — an audio-only nurse call decades before the intercom became standard hospital equipment.

interactions.title: Workshop ears
interactions.description: Since the receiver simply made one room audible in another over house wiring, a worker could leave the transmitter by a noisy machine or a doorbell and listen for trouble from the next room while working — using the device purely as a one-way room monitor.
